sections.plain_summary: Coeliac disease is an autoimmune condition in which eating gluten — a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye — triggers the immune system to attack the lining of the small intestine. Over time this damages the tiny finger-like projections (villi) that absorb nutrients, leading to malabsorption and a wide range of digestive and non-digestive symptoms. It is lifelong, but the intestine can heal substantially on a strict gluten-free diet.
sections.detailed_summary:
Coeliac disease is a systemic autoimmune disorder driven by an abnormal immune response to dietary gluten in genetically susceptible individuals (primarily those carrying HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 gene variants). When gluten-derived peptides cross the intestinal epithelium, they are deamidated by the enzyme tissue transglutaminase (tTG) and presented to T cells, triggering both adaptive and innate immune responses. The resulting chronic inflammation leads to villous atrophy — flattening of the intestinal villi — crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, collectively reducing the absorptive surface area of the small bowel.

The downstream consequences extend far beyond digestion. Malabsorption of iron, folate, calcium, vitamin D, B12, zinc, and fat-soluble vitamins can cause anaemia, osteoporosis, neurological symptoms, and reproductive complications. The condition can present at any age, and symptom patterns vary widely — from classic diarrhoea and weight loss, to 'silent' disease detected incidentally, to predominantly extraintestinal presentations such as dermatitis herpetiformis or peripheral neuropathy.

Diagnosis requires active gluten consumption (not prior elimination), involves serological screening followed by duodenal biopsy, and is confirmed by histological changes. The only proven treatment is strict, lifelong gluten avoidance. While most individuals with confirmed coeliac disease experience significant mucosal healing on a gluten-free diet, complete histological recovery can take years and is not universal — and persistent or recurrent symptoms warrant investigation for ongoing gluten exposure, secondary intolerances, or complications such as refractory coeliac disease.

prevalence.summary: Coeliac disease affects roughly 1 in 100 people globally, though the majority remain undiagnosed.
prevalence.estimate: ~1% of the global population; estimated 0.7–1.4% in Europe and North America
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates come largely from serology-based population screening rather than diagnosis rates; many studies suggest fewer than 30% of those with coeliac disease have received a formal diagnosis. The condition is more common in women (approximately 2:1 female-to-male ratio), in those of Northern European ancestry, and in first-degree relatives of diagnosed individuals (approximately 10% risk). Rates appear to be rising, possibly reflecting improved detection as well as genuine increases.

subgroups.name: Classic coeliac disease
subgroups.description: The most recognisable presentation, featuring prominent gastrointestinal symptoms and evidence of malabsorption.
distinguishing_features: Chronic diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, or loose stools
Significant weight loss or failure to thrive
Abdominal distension and pain
Nutritional deficiencies on blood tests (iron, folate, B12, vitamin D)

subgroups.name: Non-classic (atypical) coeliac disease
subgroups.description: Presentation dominated by extraintestinal symptoms with minimal or absent gut symptoms, making diagnosis easy to miss.
distinguishing_features: Iron-deficiency anaemia resistant to oral supplementation
Metabolic bone disease or osteoporosis, especially in younger individuals
Neurological symptoms: peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, cognitive fog
Subfertility, recurrent miscarriage, or delayed puberty
Elevated liver enzymes without clear cause

subgroups.name: Silent coeliac disease
subgroups.description: Positive serology and villous atrophy on biopsy, but no overt or reported symptoms — often found during screening of at-risk groups.
distinguishing_features: Detected through family screening or investigation of unrelated conditions
No subjective gastrointestinal complaints despite intestinal damage
Still at risk for long-term complications including osteoporosis and lymphoma

subgroups.name: Latent coeliac disease
subgroups.description: Positive HLA genetics and serology, normal intestinal histology at time of testing — may or may not progress to active disease.
distinguishing_features: Normal or near-normal duodenal biopsy despite positive coeliac antibodies
History of previous biopsy-confirmed disease now in histological remission on a gluten-free diet
Requires monitoring rather than immediate treatment in some cases

subgroups.name: Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)
subgroups.description: The skin manifestation of coeliac disease — an intensely itchy blistering rash driven by the same gluten-triggered immune mechanism.
distinguishing_features: Symmetrical blistering rash on elbows, knees, buttocks, and scalp
IgA deposits in the dermis on skin biopsy (diagnostic)
Gut symptoms may be minimal or absent
Responds to gluten-free diet; dapsone used for acute rash control

subgroups.name: Seronegative coeliac disease
subgroups.description: Villous atrophy confirmed on biopsy but with negative standard antibody tests — requires careful differential diagnosis.
distinguishing_features: Negative tTG-IgA and EMA despite intestinal damage
May be seen in selective IgA deficiency (requires IgG-based testing)
Must exclude other causes of villous atrophy: SIBO, Giardia, autoimmune enteropathy, drug-induced
Still managed with gluten-free diet if other causes excluded

subgroups.name: Refractory coeliac disease (RCD)
subgroups.description: Persistent villous atrophy and symptoms despite strict adherence to a gluten-free diet for more than 12 months.
distinguishing_features: RCD Type I: normal intraepithelial lymphocyte phenotype — generally responds to immunosuppression
RCD Type II: aberrant clonal lymphocyte population — high risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL)
Requires specialist investigation and monitoring in a tertiary centre
Rare: estimated to affect fewer than 5% of coeliac patients

symptoms.notes: Often pale, bulky, and foul-smelling due to fat malabsorption (steatorrhoea); may alternate with constipation in some individuals
Can be severe and persistent; often worsens after gluten-containing meals but may be present even on attempted gluten restriction due to secondary intolerances
May be diffuse or periumbilical; can mimic IBS
More common during active disease; often improves with dietary adherence

symptoms.symptom: Weight loss or poor weight gain
symptoms.notes: Particularly notable in children; adults may present with or without weight loss
Often driven by iron, folate, or B12 deficiency anaemia; may persist even after dietary adherence due to slow mucosal healing
Among the most common non-gastrointestinal presentations; often resistant to oral iron supplementation until the gut heals
Can cause macrocytic anaemia; B12 deficiency may cause neurological symptoms independently

symptoms.symptom: Bone pain and reduced bone density
symptoms.notes: Driven by calcium and vitamin D malabsorption; osteopaenia or osteoporosis may be identified on DEXA scan
Can occur in peripheral joints or the spine; may precede digestive symptoms
Tingling, numbness, or burning in the hands and feet; may occur independently of nutritional deficiencies via direct immune mechanisms
A recognised but underdiagnosed neurological manifestation; characterised by gait imbalance and coordination problems; may occur without gut symptoms
Commonly reported; mechanisms may include neuroinflammation, nutritional deficiency, and disrupted gut-brain signalling
Higher prevalence in coeliac disease than the general population; may improve on a gluten-free diet
Bidirectional relationship; may partly reflect nutritional deficiencies, systemic inflammation, and the psychosocial burden of the condition
Intensely itchy, symmetrical blistering rash on extensor surfaces; the skin manifestation of coeliac disease — diagnosed by skin biopsy showing IgA deposits
Recurrent ulcers that may improve significantly on a gluten-free diet
Horizontal banding or pitting of permanent teeth, particularly if coeliac disease was active during childhood tooth development
Often mild and reversible on a gluten-free diet; coeliac disease is a recognised cause of unexplained raised ALT/AST
Associated with untreated coeliac disease in both men and women; mechanism may involve nutritional deficiencies and systemic inflammation
Particularly in children with undiagnosed disease; may be the sole presenting feature

key_patterns: Iron-deficiency anaemia that does not respond to oral iron supplementation — especially in a young woman — is a classic alert for undiagnosed coeliac disease
Multiple nutritional deficiencies simultaneously (iron + folate + vitamin D + zinc) suggests small intestinal malabsorption rather than dietary inadequacy alone
Gut symptoms that began or worsened in childhood or adolescence, particularly diarrhoea, bloating, and failure to thrive
Family history of coeliac disease combined with any unexplained gastrointestinal, neurological, dermatological, or reproductive symptoms
Positive tTG-IgA serology requires confirmation with duodenal biopsy; serology alone is not diagnostic in most guidelines
Normal coeliac serology does not rule out disease if the person has already reduced gluten intake before testing — gluten must be consumed for accurate results

at_home.name: Dietary symptom journal
at_home.description: Tracking meals alongside symptoms — including timing of gluten ingestion relative to symptom onset — can reveal a consistent pattern that supports pursuing formal testing. This is observational only and cannot confirm coeliac disease.
at_home.safety_notes: Do not begin a gluten-free diet before completing all diagnostic testing. Gluten elimination before testing will normalise serology and may normalise biopsy findings, making diagnosis impossible without a formal gluten challenge.

at_home.name: Over-the-counter coeliac antibody test
at_home.description: Home finger-prick tests measuring tTG-IgA are available in some countries. A positive result warrants urgent follow-up with a clinician. A negative result is not definitive, particularly if gluten intake has been low or the person has IgA deficiency.
at_home.safety_notes: These tests are screening tools only — not diagnostic. They require active gluten consumption to be reliable. A positive result should not prompt self-directed dietary change before biopsy confirmation.

clinical.name: Tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA)
clinical.description: The primary serological screening test for coeliac disease. Measures IgA antibodies against tissue transglutaminase. Sensitivity approximately 95% and specificity approximately 95% when performed on a gluten-containing diet. Should always be interpreted alongside total serum IgA to exclude selective IgA deficiency.
clinical.availability: Orderable by GPs and most primary care physicians. Widely available on the NHS and equivalent national health systems.

clinical.name: Total serum IgA
clinical.description: Measured alongside tTG-IgA to detect selective IgA deficiency (present in ~2–3% of coeliac patients), which would produce a falsely negative tTG-IgA result. If IgA deficient, IgG-based tests (tTG-IgG, DGP-IgG) are used instead.
clinical.availability: Routinely ordered alongside coeliac serology; available from GP.

clinical.name: Deamidated gliadin peptide antibodies (DGP-IgA and DGP-IgG)
clinical.description: Particularly useful in children under 2 years (in whom tTG-IgA may be less sensitive), and in individuals with IgA deficiency. Also used as part of confirmatory panels.
clinical.availability: Available from GP or gastroenterology referral; not always included in standard panels — may need to be specifically requested.

clinical.name: Endomysial antibody (EMA-IgA)
clinical.description: Highly specific (~99%) for coeliac disease when positive. Used as a confirmatory test alongside tTG-IgA. Operator-dependent and performed less commonly due to cost and technical requirements.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterology or specialist laboratories; less commonly ordered in primary care.

clinical.name: Duodenal biopsy via upper endoscopy (OGD)
clinical.description: The definitive diagnostic test in most guidelines. Multiple biopsies taken from the second and third parts of the duodenum and duodenal bulb during an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy. Histology is graded using the Marsh classification (Marsh 3a–3c representing progressive villous atrophy). Requires active gluten consumption for accurate results.
clinical.availability: Performed by gastroenterologists following positive serology. Available through NHS gastroenterology referral and private endoscopy units. Some European guidelines now allow diagnosis without biopsy in adults with tTG-IgA >10× upper limit of normal plus positive EMA.

clinical.name: HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genetic testing
clinical.description: Tests for the HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8 gene variants present in ~95% of coeliac patients. A negative result has very high negative predictive value and can effectively rule out coeliac disease. A positive result does not confirm disease (30–40% of the general population carry these variants). Useful when testing cannot be completed on a gluten-containing diet, or to clarify ambiguous cases.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterology or genetics services; also available privately. Not routinely ordered in primary care but valuable in complex cases.

clinical.name: Coeliac-specific small bowel capsule endoscopy
clinical.description: A swallowable camera that images the entire small intestine. Useful when standard endoscopy is inconclusive or for assessing extent of disease, but not a first-line diagnostic tool.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist gastroenterology centres; not widely used for primary diagnosis.

clinical.name: DEXA scan (bone density)
clinical.description: Assesses bone mineral density to detect osteopaenia or osteoporosis resulting from prolonged calcium and vitamin D malabsorption. Recommended at diagnosis in adults.
clinical.availability: Available through GP or rheumatology/gastroenterology referral; widely available.

clinical.name: Nutritional blood panel
clinical.description: Full blood count, ferritin, serum iron, folate, B12, vitamin D, calcium, zinc, and liver function tests. These tests characterise the degree of malabsorption and guide supplementation. Abnormal results provide supporting evidence for active disease.
clinical.availability: Orderable by GP; included in most coeliac disease diagnostic workups.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease is frequently misdiagnosed as IBS due to overlapping symptoms of bloating, diarrhoea, and abdominal pain. Studies suggest 1–5% of people with an IBS diagnosis have undetected coeliac disease. All IBS guidelines recommend coeliac serology before diagnosis is confirmed.

related_conditions.name: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)
related_conditions.explanation: NCGS produces gluten-related symptoms without the autoimmune damage or specific antibodies of coeliac disease. Distinguishing the two requires testing on a gluten-containing diet before any dietary change. NCGS remains a diagnosis of exclusion after coeliac disease has been ruled out.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Villous atrophy and altered intestinal motility in coeliac disease can predispose to SIBO. SIBO can also cause persistent symptoms in people with coeliac disease who are already on a gluten-free diet, and may be an underrecognised cause of non-responsive coeliac disease.

related_conditions.name: Lactase deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Secondary lactase deficiency is common in active coeliac disease, as lactase enzymes are located on the tips of intestinal villi and are lost during villous atrophy. Lactose intolerance symptoms often resolve or improve substantially after mucosal healing on a gluten-free diet.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Active coeliac disease is associated with significant alterations in gut microbiome composition, including reduced microbial diversity. Dysbiosis may contribute to persistent symptoms and may not fully normalise even on a long-term gluten-free diet.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's thyroiditis and Graves' disease) occurs at significantly elevated rates in people with coeliac disease, reflecting shared autoimmune genetic susceptibility. Thyroid function should be tested in those newly diagnosed with coeliac disease.

related_conditions.name: Microscopic colitis
related_conditions.explanation: Microscopic colitis occurs at higher rates in people with coeliac disease than the general population. It can cause persistent watery diarrhoea even after gluten elimination, making it an important cause of non-responsive coeliac disease to exclude.

related_conditions.name: Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's disease / ulcerative colitis)
related_conditions.explanation: There is a modest but established increased prevalence of IBD in coeliac disease patients and vice versa. The conditions can coexist, and IBD should be considered when symptoms persist despite gluten-free diet adherence.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cell activation may be elevated in active and treated coeliac disease. MCAS can produce multi-system symptoms overlapping with coeliac disease, and may be a contributor to persistent symptoms in some individuals on a strict gluten-free diet.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Intestinal damage in coeliac disease may impair the production of diamine oxidase (DAO), the enzyme responsible for breaking down dietary histamine, potentially contributing to histamine intolerance as a secondary condition.

related_conditions.name: Eosinophilic GI disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Eosinophilic oesophagitis and eosinophilic gastroenteritis can produce overlapping symptoms and histological abnormalities. Coeliac disease and eosinophilic disorders can also coexist, complicating clinical interpretation.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety is significantly more prevalent in people with coeliac disease, reflecting both the psychosocial burden of managing a strict lifelong diet and potential direct effects of gut-brain axis disruption and systemic inflammation.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Higher rates of depression are documented in coeliac disease, particularly before diagnosis and during periods of poor dietary adherence. Nutritional deficiencies (B12, folate, vitamin D) and chronic inflammation may contribute directly.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Epidemiological studies suggest a higher prevalence of coeliac disease in women with endometriosis and vice versa. Shared mechanisms may include systemic inflammation and immune dysregulation.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: People with hEDS report higher rates of digestive conditions including coeliac disease. The mechanistic link is not fully established, though connective tissue abnormalities affecting the gut wall and shared immune dysregulation are proposed contributors.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: POTS and dysautonomia appear more frequently in people with coeliac disease than the general population. Autonomic neuropathy related to gluten immune responses and nutritional deficiencies may play a role.

related_conditions.name: Osteoporosis
related_conditions.explanation: Calcium and vitamin D malabsorption in uncontrolled or late-diagnosed coeliac disease leads to significant bone mineral density loss. This is one of the most clinically important long-term complications and warrants DEXA scanning at diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Type 1 diabetes
related_conditions.explanation: Type 1 diabetes and coeliac disease share HLA genetic risk loci (particularly HLA-DQ2/DQ8) and co-occur at significantly elevated rates. Screening for coeliac disease is recommended in all individuals with type 1 diabetes.

related_conditions.name: Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease occurs in approximately 5–12% of people with Down syndrome — 5–10 times the general population rate — and is often silent or atypically presented, making routine screening important in this group.

related_conditions.name: Turner syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Turner syndrome is associated with a significantly elevated prevalence of coeliac disease, and routine screening is recommended by endocrine and genetics guidelines.

strategies.name: Strict lifelong gluten-free diet
strategies.description: The only proven treatment for coeliac disease. Involves the complete elimination of wheat, barley, rye, and any products derived from them. Even small amounts of gluten (as low as 10–50mg/day) can sustain intestinal inflammation. Oats are tolerated by most — but not all — coeliac patients; certified gluten-free oats are preferred.
strategies.notes: This is not optional or dose-dependent in the way some intolerances are — it is a medical intervention. Strict adherence over months to years allows mucosal healing and resolution of most symptoms and complications. Cross-contamination in shared kitchens, toasters, cutting boards, and frying oil is a common and underestimated source of ongoing exposure.

strategies.name: Working with a FODMAP-trained or coeliac-specialist dietitian
strategies.description: A dietitian experienced in coeliac disease can help identify hidden gluten sources, assess nutritional adequacy of the gluten-free diet (which is often low in fibre, B vitamins, and iron), manage secondary intolerances that emerge during healing, and support safe reintroduction of oats.
strategies.notes: Many people self-manage the gluten-free diet without dietetic support and remain nutritionally deficient or continue to consume hidden gluten. Annual dietitian review is recommended in specialist guidelines.

strategies.name: Nutritional supplementation (targeted deficiencies)
strategies.description: At diagnosis, deficiencies in iron, folate, vitamin B12, vitamin D, calcium, and zinc are common and should be corrected with supplementation while the gut heals. Supplementation requirements typically reduce as mucosal recovery progresses, but some individuals require ongoing support.
strategies.notes: Supplementation should be guided by blood test results rather than blanket dosing. Intravenous iron may be needed if oral supplementation is not absorbed effectively in the early stages of recovery.

strategies.name: Temporary lactose restriction during mucosal healing
strategies.description: Secondary lactase deficiency is common in active coeliac disease. Reducing dairy intake or using lactase supplements during the healing phase may reduce bloating, diarrhoea, and cramp. Most individuals can reintroduce dairy once the intestinal lining has recovered.
strategies.notes: Avoiding dairy permanently is not necessary for most — and may worsen calcium deficiency. Reassessment after 6–12 months on a gluten-free diet is recommended.

strategies.name: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation for bone protection
strategies.description: Given the high prevalence of reduced bone density at diagnosis, calcium and vitamin D supplementation alongside dietary optimisation is commonly recommended. DEXA scanning guides intensity of intervention.
strategies.notes: Weight-bearing exercise also contributes to bone density maintenance. Some individuals may need bisphosphonate therapy — this should be assessed by a specialist after gut healing, as bone density may improve substantially on the gluten-free diet alone.

strategies.name: Cross-contamination prevention in the home kitchen
strategies.description: Using dedicated gluten-free toasters, cutting boards, colanders, and storage containers; thoroughly cleaning shared surfaces and cookware; using separate condiment jars to prevent crumb contamination. These measures are particularly important in households where not all members are gluten-free.
strategies.notes: Cross-contamination is one of the most commonly overlooked sources of ongoing gluten exposure, particularly in people who believe they are strictly gluten-free but have persistent serology or symptoms.

strategies.name: Eating out safely — research and communication
strategies.description: Identifying coeliac-aware restaurants (those with dedicated gluten-free fryers, preparation areas, and staff training), communicating the medical necessity of strict avoidance (not a lifestyle preference), and using coeliac-specific dining apps and resources.
strategies.notes: Eating out carries significantly higher gluten exposure risk. Some individuals with coeliac disease choose to eat out only at certified safe venues, particularly during the mucosal healing phase.

strategies.name: Annual monitoring with serology and nutritional bloods
strategies.description: Ongoing monitoring of tTG-IgA antibody levels helps confirm dietary adherence and detect ongoing exposure. Nutritional panels track deficiency resolution and identify new gaps. Some guidelines also recommend periodic repeat biopsy to assess mucosal healing.
strategies.notes: Falling but persistently positive serology after 12 months on a gluten-free diet suggests continued gluten exposure — deliberate, inadvertent, or via cross-contamination — and warrants detailed dietetic review.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet for residual functional symptoms
strategies.description: In individuals who have achieved mucosal healing on a gluten-free diet but still experience bloating, gas, or altered bowel habits, a trial of a low-FODMAP diet (supervised by a dietitian) may help identify additional dietary triggers such as fructose, lactose, or polyols.
strategies.notes: The low-FODMAP diet should not be implemented long-term without professional guidance; it restricts many prebiotic foods and may worsen gut microbiome diversity if maintained without a planned reintroduction phase.

strategies.name: Psychological support and dietary coaching
strategies.description: Managing a strict lifelong elimination diet has measurable impacts on social functioning, anxiety around food, and quality of life. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), acceptance-based approaches, and peer support groups can help people develop sustainable strategies.
strategies.notes: Disordered eating patterns can emerge around the gluten-free diet, particularly in adolescents and young adults. Psychological support alongside dietetic management is recommended when this is suspected.

things_to_avoid: Beginning a gluten-free diet before completing serology and biopsy — this makes accurate diagnosis impossible and may require a prolonged and unpleasant gluten challenge to reinstate
Assuming 'gluten-free' labelled products are safe without checking for cross-contamination warnings — manufacturing standards vary between countries
Eating oats that are not certified gluten-free — standard oats are heavily cross-contaminated with wheat in most supply chains
Sharing toasters, colanders, wooden spoons, or cutting boards with gluten-containing products — these are common vectors for trace contamination
Relying on a gluten-free diet as sole management without monitoring nutritional status — the GF diet is often deficient in fibre, B vitamins, and iron
Concluding that symptoms have resolved and relaxing dietary adherence — antibody levels and intestinal healing may continue to deteriorate before subjective symptoms return
Self-diagnosing coeliac disease based on symptom response to a gluten-free diet — this pattern is indistinguishable from NCGS or FODMAP sensitivity without confirmed serology and biopsy

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Starting a gluten-free diet before testing does not just make diagnosis harder — it can make it impossible without subjecting yourself to weeks of deliberate gluten consumption (a gluten challenge), which is genuinely unpleasant and medically complex.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People feel better after removing gluten and do not want to reintroduce it. The instinct is to prioritise feeling well over getting a confirmed diagnosis.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who get a confirmed biopsy diagnosis have a legitimate medical basis for strict avoidance, can access dietetic support, monitoring, and screening of family members, and are taken seriously by clinicians and employers. Those who self-diagnose often get inconsistent management and drift back toward gluten over time without any safety net.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many people diagnosed with coeliac disease are not actually following a strict gluten-free diet — they are following a low-gluten diet, which is not the same thing and does not protect the intestine.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: A low-gluten diet often reduces symptoms significantly, creating a false sense of compliance. Social situations, eating out, and label reading are effortful, and it is easy to underestimate the impact of trace amounts.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who achieve strict adherence — verified by persistently normalised tTG-IgA and confirmed mucosal healing on follow-up biopsy — have the best long-term outcomes including reduced cancer risk, resolved nutritional deficiencies, and improved bone density.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Mucosal healing after starting a gluten-free diet is slow and often incomplete — a significant proportion of adults with coeliac disease still have abnormal biopsy findings after 2 years on a strict diet.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People assume that once they are symptom-free and feel better, the gut has healed. Subjective improvement precedes histological recovery by months to years.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who understand this are more likely to pursue follow-up biopsies or serology monitoring, to avoid relaxing dietary vigilance prematurely, and to investigate genuine non-responsiveness with a clinician rather than accepting persistent symptoms as inevitable.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The long-term complication risk — particularly for intestinal lymphoma (EATL) — is meaningfully elevated in people with untreated or poorly controlled coeliac disease. It is not vanishingly rare.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Lymphoma feels remote and abstract. People prioritise day-to-day quality of life over long-term risk. Some dismiss it as alarmism.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Strict dietary adherence is the most effective risk-reduction strategy available, and the relative risk of EATL returns toward population baseline in those with documented mucosal healing. This is a concrete, measurable reason to pursue real compliance rather than approximate compliance.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A gluten-free diet is not nutritionally superior by default — it is often less nutritionally complete than a standard diet. Many gluten-free substitute products are low in fibre, high in refined starch, and have minimal B-vitamin fortification compared to their wheat-based equivalents.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People assume 'free from' foods are healthy and feel virtuous about dietary restriction. Food marketing reinforces this.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who work with a dietitian to build a whole-food gluten-free diet — emphasising naturally gluten-free grains (quinoa, buckwheat, oats if tolerated, rice), legumes, and vegetables — achieve better nutritional outcomes than those who rely on substitute products.

key_levers.lever: Complete, confirmed diagnosis before dietary change
key_levers.why_it_matters: A confirmed diagnosis — serology plus biopsy — is the gateway to appropriate monitoring, family screening, dietetic support, and clinical follow-up. Without it, management is arbitrary and long-term risk is unquantified.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request tTG-IgA and total IgA from a GP before making any dietary changes. If the result is positive or borderline, request referral to a gastroenterologist for endoscopy.
key_levers.timeline: Diagnosis typically completed within 1–3 months if the referral pathway is followed promptly

key_levers.lever: Strict, sustained gluten-free diet with professional dietetic input
key_levers.why_it_matters: This is the only intervention that halts intestinal damage, allows mucosal recovery, reverses nutritional deficiencies, and reduces long-term complication risk. Partial adherence does not achieve these goals.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek referral to or self-refer to a registered dietitian with coeliac disease experience. Complete a detailed dietary audit to identify hidden gluten sources and cross-contamination risks.
key_levers.timeline: Antibody levels typically normalise within 6–12 months; mucosal healing takes 1–3 years in most adults, longer in some

key_levers.why_it_matters: Active nutritional deficiencies — particularly iron, B12, folate, and vitamin D — drive many of the most debilitating symptoms of coeliac disease including fatigue, cognitive fog, mood disturbance, and bone pain. These are correctable.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a full nutritional panel (ferritin, B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium, zinc, LFTs, FBC) at diagnosis and at 6–12 month intervals. Begin targeted supplementation based on results.
key_levers.timeline: Most deficiencies improve within 3–6 months of supplementation, though iron repletion may take longer if absorption remains impaired

key_levers.why_it_matters: Ongoing serology monitoring detects silent ongoing gluten exposure before complications develop. Nutritional monitoring catches new deficiencies. Monitoring maintains clinical engagement and accountability for adherence.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask the diagnosing gastroenterologist to set up an annual review — or at minimum arrange annual tTG-IgA and nutritional bloods through a GP. Many coeliac societies offer monitoring checklists.
key_levers.timeline: Lifelong; frequency may reduce once stable

key_levers.why_it_matters: First-degree relatives of coeliac patients have approximately a 10% risk of coeliac disease — far exceeding population prevalence. Many relatives are asymptomatic but have active intestinal damage. Screening prevents delayed diagnosis.
key_levers.how_to_start: Inform first-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) of the diagnosis and the recommendation to be tested. They should request tTG-IgA from their own GP while on a gluten-containing diet.
key_levers.timeline: One-off test; may be repeated periodically if negative, as disease can develop at any age

key_levers.why_it_matters: Osteopaenia and osteoporosis are common at coeliac diagnosis due to prolonged calcium and vitamin D malabsorption. Untreated, this leads to fracture risk. A gluten-free diet allows partial bone density recovery, and active management accelerates this.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a DEXA scan at diagnosis. Begin calcium and vitamin D supplementation if dietary intake is insufficient. Incorporate weight-bearing exercise.
key_levers.timeline: Bone density can improve substantially over 1–2 years on a strict gluten-free diet with appropriate supplementation

common_mistakes: Self-diagnosing based on symptom improvement on a gluten-free diet and never completing formal testing — this forecloses accurate diagnosis and appropriate monitoring
Believing that 'mostly gluten-free' or 'low gluten' is adequate — the threshold for intestinal damage is very low, and partial restriction does not protect the gut
Not reading ingredient labels on processed foods, medications, supplements, and cosmetics — gluten appears under many names and in unexpected products
Assuming a gluten-free diet is automatically nutritionally complete — without dietetic guidance, it is often fibre-poor and micronutrient-deficient
Discontinuing serology monitoring once symptoms resolve — antibody levels and mucosal healing must be actively confirmed, not assumed
Not informing family members of the diagnosis — delayed diagnosis in relatives means years of preventable intestinal damage
Treating oat tolerance as universal — a minority of people with coeliac disease react to avenin in oats and should trial certified gluten-free oats gradually under dietetic supervision

specialists.role: Performs the diagnostic endoscopy and biopsy, interprets histological results, manages complications including non-responsive disease, and coordinates specialist follow-up. Essential for initial diagnosis and for complex or refractory cases.
specialists.notes: Not all gastroenterologists have specialist coeliac expertise. Those working in tertiary centres or with a specific interest in small bowel disease and coeliac disease will have more experience with complex presentations and refractory disease.

specialists.role: The most practically important ongoing support for most people with coeliac disease. Provides detailed dietary audit, identifies hidden gluten sources, addresses nutritional gaps in the gluten-free diet, manages secondary intolerances, and supports food anxiety and social eating challenges.
specialists.notes: Many people with coeliac disease never see a dietitian after diagnosis and remain on nutritionally inadequate or contaminated diets. Self-referral to a private dietitian is an option when NHS or insurance pathways are slow. Look specifically for dietitians with gastroenterology or coeliac disease experience rather than general practice dietitians.

specialists.role: Orders initial serology, refers for endoscopy, provides annual monitoring bloods, prescribes nutritional supplements, and coordinates care. In the UK, some GPs prescribe gluten-free staple foods on the NHS.
specialists.notes: Many GPs have limited detailed knowledge of coeliac disease beyond initial diagnosis. Patients who understand their own monitoring needs and advocate for annual blood tests and dietetic referral tend to receive better ongoing care.

specialists.role: Specialist management for children and adolescents, including diagnosis, growth monitoring, support for managing the diet in school environments, and management of transition to adult services.
specialists.notes: Some European guidelines allow diagnosis in children without biopsy when tTG-IgA is very strongly elevated — a paediatric gastroenterologist will assess whether this applies.

specialists.role: Relevant for individuals with neurological manifestations including gluten ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, or cognitive symptoms. The Gluten Neuropathy Clinic in Sheffield, UK, pioneered much of the research in this area.
specialists.notes: Mainstream neurologists may not be familiar with gluten neurology as a distinct entity. Seeking a neurologist with a specific interest in immune-mediated neuropathies or those aware of the gluten neurology literature is advisable.

specialists.role: Diagnoses dermatitis herpetiformis via skin biopsy and direct immunofluorescence. Manages the rash with dapsone or other agents while the gluten-free diet takes effect.
specialists.notes: Diagnosis of DH must be made by a dermatologist — it cannot be confirmed from serology or gut biopsy alone. A dermatologist with experience in blistering skin conditions is needed.

specialists.role: Manages significant osteoporosis or fracture risk, assesses whether bisphosphonates are needed once mucosal healing is underway, and co-manages skeletal complications.
specialists.notes: Referral is typically initiated following DEXA scan results rather than directly at diagnosis.

specialists.type: Functional medicine or integrative medicine practitioner
specialists.role: May be useful for exploring secondary intolerances, gut dysbiosis, SIBO, or persistent symptoms despite a strict gluten-free diet — areas that mainstream gastroenterology may not investigate proactively.
specialists.notes: Quality varies considerably in this field. The most useful practitioners are those who use evidence-based functional testing (breath tests, validated stool analysis) rather than unvalidated panels.

patient_communities.name: r/Celiac
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community of people with diagnosed coeliac disease sharing experiences of managing the diet, eating out safely, navigating diagnosis, and dealing with symptoms. Generally evidence-informed and strict about the distinction between coeliac disease and NCGS.

patient_communities.name: Coeliac UK Community
patient_communities.notes: The UK's leading coeliac charity, with a member community forum, food and ingredient checker app, restaurant guide, and extensive evidence-based resources. Annual membership provides access to the full food database.

patient_communities.name: Beyond Celiac Community
patient_communities.notes: US-focused patient advocacy and research organisation with community forums, research updates, and practical guides for newly diagnosed individuals and those managing complex situations.

patient_communities.name: Celiac Disease Foundation
patient_communities.notes: Major US advocacy organisation with patient resources, research news, and a private Facebook community. Provides updates on emerging treatments and clinical trials.

patient_communities.name: Gluten Free Watchdog Community
patient_communities.notes: Independent testing organisation that evaluates gluten-free product labels for accuracy. Particularly valuable for people who need to verify that products marketed as gluten-free actually meet safe thresholds.

learning_resources.title: Coeliac UK — Newly Diagnosed
learning_resources.description: Clear, evidence-based introductory resource covering what coeliac disease is, how it is diagnosed, how to start a gluten-free diet, and where to get support. A reliable first port of call for people newly diagnosed or suspected of having the condition.

learning_resources.title: Beyond Celiac — About Celiac Disease
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, patient-centred website covering diagnosis, the gluten-free diet, symptoms, related conditions, and emerging research. Particularly strong on the science of coeliac disease presented accessibly.

learning_resources.title: Real Life with Celiac Disease
learning_resources.description: A practical guide written by specialists at the Celiac Center at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. Covers the full spectrum from diagnosis to managing social situations, eating out, nutritional pitfalls, and dealing with persistent symptoms. Well regarded in both patient and clinical communities.

learning_resources.title: The Gluten Lie (critical perspective)
learning_resources.description: A balanced, sceptical examination of gluten fear in popular culture. Does not deny coeliac disease — takes it seriously — but challenges the broader anti-gluten wellness narrative. Useful for people trying to understand what is science and what is marketing.

learning_resources.title: Celiac Disease: A Hidden Epidemic
learning_resources.author_or_source: Peter H.R. Green and Rory Jones
learning_resources.description: Written by the director of the Celiac Disease Center at Columbia University. A thorough overview of coeliac disease from a medical perspective that is still accessible to patients. Covers diagnosis, treatment, complications, and the spectrum of presentations. Slightly dated but foundationally sound.

learning_resources.title: Gluten Free Watchdog
learning_resources.description: Independent testing of gluten-free products to verify label accuracy. Essential reading for people who need to know whether the products they are relying on genuinely contain less than 20 ppm gluten. Subscription-based but detailed.

learning_resources.title: Coeliac Disease: Management (NICE guideline NG20)
learning_resources.author_or_source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE)
learning_resources.description: The UK's official clinical guideline for coeliac disease management. Useful for patients who want to understand what standards of care they can reasonably expect from clinicians and how to advocate for appropriate monitoring and follow-up.

learning_resources.title: The Gluten Connection Podcast
learning_resources.description: Interviews with researchers, clinicians, and patient advocates covering coeliac disease research, management, and lived experience. Particularly strong for staying updated on emerging treatments and clinical trial developments.

learning_resources.title: Spectrum of Gluten-Related Disorders (Sapone et al., 2012)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Sapone A et al., BMC Medicine
learning_resources.description: The foundational paper distinguishing coeliac disease, wheat allergy, and non-coeliac gluten sensitivity. Essential reading for understanding why these conditions are distinct and how to approach differential diagnosis. Freely accessible.

sections.relevance_signal: **Diarrhoea, bloating, fatigue, and anaemia** — especially if northern European heritage. Symptoms improve on gluten-free diet. May have no gut symptoms at all (silent coeliac). Do not start gluten-free diet before testing.
